bbox.detailAbout: Draw rectangles around objects in your images to teach a model where they are. This is the most popular approach for object detection — fast to annotate, easy to train, and supported by virtually every detection framework.
bbox.canDo1: Locate and count objects in photos or video frames
bbox.canDo2: Train real-time detectors like YOLO or SSD
bbox.canDo3: Build inventory, quality inspection, or surveillance systems
bbox.canDo4: Export to COCO, Pascal VOC, YOLO, and other standard formats
bbox.exampleUse: A warehouse team annotates boxes on conveyor belt images. After training a YOLO model, cameras count packages automatically and flag any that fall off the belt.

mask.detailAbout: Paint over regions pixel by pixel to classify every part of an image. Unlike boxes or polygons, masks capture exact shapes — perfect when precision matters more than speed.
mask.canDo1: Classify every pixel into categories (sky, road, building…)
mask.canDo2: Create training data for medical imaging or satellite analysis
mask.canDo3: Separate foreground from background with pixel-perfect accuracy
mask.canDo4: Generate segmentation maps for autonomous driving or robotics
mask.exampleUse: A medical research lab paints tumor regions on MRI scans. The trained model highlights suspicious areas for radiologists, cutting review time in half.

polygon.detailAbout: Trace the outline of objects by placing points that form a polygon. It's lighter and faster than pixel masks while still capturing precise shapes — a great middle ground between boxes and full masks.
polygon.canDo1: Outline irregular objects with precise boundaries
polygon.canDo2: Annotate faster than pixel-level masks with similar accuracy
polygon.canDo3: Train instance or semantic segmentation models
polygon.canDo4: Export to COCO polygon format or convert to masks on the fly
polygon.exampleUse: An agriculture startup traces crop rows and weed patches in drone images. The polygon annotations train a model that guides a robotic sprayer to target only the weeds.

keypoints.detailAbout: Place connected points on objects to describe their structure — like marking joints on a body or corners on a face. The connections between points capture how parts relate to each other.
keypoints.canDo1: Annotate body poses for fitness, sports, or ergonomics apps
keypoints.canDo2: Mark facial landmarks for expression or identity analysis
keypoints.canDo3: Define articulated structures on animals, hands, or machines
keypoints.canDo4: Train pose estimation models like OpenPose or HRNet
keypoints.exampleUse: A physiotherapy clinic marks shoulder, elbow, and wrist joints on exercise videos. The trained model measures joint angles in real time, giving patients instant feedback on their form.

landmarks.detailAbout: Drop individual points on images to mark locations of interest — no connections, no shapes, just positions. Use them for counting, centering, or pinpointing anything.
landmarks.canDo1: Count objects by marking their centers (cells, trees, people…)
landmarks.canDo2: Mark reference points for alignment or measurement
landmarks.canDo3: Create point-based heatmaps for density estimation
landmarks.canDo4: Train lightweight point-detection or counting models
landmarks.exampleUse: A biologist drops a point on every cell nucleus in microscope slides. The trained model counts cells across thousands of images overnight, replacing days of manual work.

obb.detailAbout: Draw rotated rectangles that tilt to match the angle of objects. Regular boxes waste space on tilted items — oriented boxes fit tightly around ships, text lines, vehicles seen from above, and more.
obb.canDo1: Detect objects at any angle in aerial or satellite images
obb.canDo2: Annotate rotated text, documents, or license plates
obb.canDo4: Improve accuracy on scenes where objects aren't axis-aligned
obb.exampleUse: A port authority annotates cargo ships in satellite photos with rotated boxes. The model detects ships regardless of heading, feeding an automated traffic monitoring dashboard.

classification.detailAbout: Assign one label to each image — the simplest form of annotation. No drawing required: just look at the image and pick the right category. Great for sorting, filtering, or building quick classifiers.
classification.canDo1: Sort images into categories (good/bad, cat/dog, type A/B…)
classification.canDo2: Build product categorization or content moderation systems
classification.canDo3: Train classifiers like ResNet, EfficientNet, or MobileNet
classification.canDo4: Get started quickly — no drawing tools needed
classification.exampleUse: An e-commerce team labels product photos as 'front', 'side', or 'detail'. The trained model auto-sorts new uploads into the right gallery slots on the website.

multi-label-classification.detailAbout: Assign multiple labels to a single image when it belongs to more than one category. Unlike simple classification, each image can carry several tags at once — like 'outdoor', 'sunny', and 'dog' all together.
multi-label-classification.canDo1: Tag images with all applicable attributes at once
multi-label-classification.canDo2: Build multi-tag search or recommendation systems
multi-label-classification.canDo3: Create rich metadata for image libraries or catalogs
multi-label-classification.canDo4: Train multi-output classifiers for complex categorization
multi-label-classification.exampleUse: A fashion retailer tags clothing photos with style, color, season, and occasion. Shoppers search 'red casual summer' and the model returns matching items instantly.

speech-recognition.detailAbout: Transcribe what people say in audio recordings. You listen to clips and type the exact words spoken, creating training data for automatic speech recognition systems.
speech-recognition.canDo1: Transcribe audio clips into accurate text
speech-recognition.canDo2: Build subtitle generators or voice assistants
speech-recognition.canDo3: Create datasets for speech-to-text models like Whisper
speech-recognition.canDo4: Handle multiple speakers, accents, and languages
speech-recognition.exampleUse: A call center transcribes thousands of customer calls. The trained ASR model converts live calls to text in real time, enabling automatic sentiment analysis and keyword alerts.

audio-classification.detailAbout: Listen to audio clips and assign a category to each one. No timeline editing — just pick the right label for the whole recording. Useful for sorting sounds, identifying genres, or detecting specific audio types.
audio-classification.canDo1: Classify audio clips by type, genre, or content
audio-classification.canDo2: Build sound recognition systems for smart devices
audio-classification.canDo3: Create training data for environmental sound models
audio-classification.canDo4: Sort music, speech, noise, or any audio category
audio-classification.exampleUse: A smart home company classifies recordings as 'glass breaking', 'dog barking', 'doorbell', or 'silence'. The model runs on a home hub, triggering alerts for each sound type.

sound-event-detection.detailAbout: Mark when specific sounds happen in a recording — their start time, end time, and type. Unlike classification (one label per clip), here you pinpoint every event along the timeline.
sound-event-detection.canDo1: Locate and label events in long audio recordings
sound-event-detection.canDo2: Build real-time monitoring for security or industry
sound-event-detection.canDo3: Annotate overlapping events in complex soundscapes
sound-event-detection.canDo4: Train temporal event detection models like CRNN or SED-CNN
sound-event-detection.exampleUse: A wildlife conservation team marks bird calls, chainsaw sounds, and vehicle noise in jungle recordings. The trained model detects illegal logging activity in near real-time from remote microphones.

tts-recording.detailAbout: A guided recording studio for building high-quality TTS datasets. Load or generate sentences, record them one by one with real-time quality monitoring, and export in LJSpeech format for training models like Piper, Coqui TTS, or VITS.
tts-recording.canDo1: Record phonetically balanced sentences guided step-by-step
tts-recording.canDo2: Generate sentence lists with AI or load from file
tts-recording.canDo3: Monitor recording quality in real-time (volume, clipping, noise)
tts-recording.canDo4: Export complete datasets in LJSpeech format
tts-recording.exampleUse: A voice actor records 500 sentences in a quiet studio using the guided interface. The dataset is exported in LJSpeech format and used to fine-tune a Piper TTS model that reads news articles aloud in their voice.

timeseries-classification.detailAbout: Look at an entire time series and assign it a category. Each series gets one label based on its overall shape or behavior — no need to mark specific time ranges.
timeseries-classification.canDo1: Classify sensor readings, signals, or sequences as a whole
timeseries-classification.canDo2: Build diagnostic tools for medical signals (ECG, EEG…)
timeseries-classification.canDo3: Recognize gestures or movements from accelerometer data
timeseries-classification.canDo4: Train models like InceptionTime, ROCKET, or ResNet-TS
timeseries-classification.exampleUse: A cardiology department labels ECG recordings as 'normal', 'arrhythmia', or 'atrial fibrillation'. The trained model screens incoming ECGs and flags abnormal cases for immediate doctor review.

timeseries-forecasting.detailAbout: Mark the historical window and the prediction horizon on your time series. The model will learn to look back at past values and predict what comes next — sales, temperatures, stock levels, or any measurable quantity.
timeseries-forecasting.canDo1: Define training windows for past-to-future prediction
timeseries-forecasting.canDo2: Build demand, weather, or financial forecasting models
timeseries-forecasting.canDo3: Experiment with different look-back and horizon lengths
timeseries-forecasting.canDo4: Train models like LSTM, Transformer, or N-BEATS
timeseries-forecasting.exampleUse: An energy company marks 7-day historical windows and 24-hour prediction horizons on electricity demand data. The trained model forecasts next-day load, optimizing power generation schedules.

anomaly-detection.detailAbout: Scan time series and mark the points or regions that look unusual. Your annotations teach a model what 'normal' looks like so it can flag deviations — spikes, drops, drift, or any unexpected behavior.
anomaly-detection.canDo1: Mark anomalous points or ranges in sensor data
anomaly-detection.canDo2: Build predictive maintenance or fraud detection systems
anomaly-detection.canDo3: Monitor health metrics, network traffic, or equipment
anomaly-detection.canDo4: Train autoencoders, isolation forests, or LSTM anomaly detectors
anomaly-detection.exampleUse: A factory annotates vibration sensor data, marking the moments before machine breakdowns. The model learns the early warning signs and alerts maintenance crews hours before failures happen.

timeseries-segmentation.detailAbout: Divide a time series into segments and label each one. Think of it like coloring different parts of a timeline — one region is 'running', another is 'resting', another is 'sleeping'.
timeseries-segmentation.canDo1: Split time series into meaningful labeled regions
timeseries-segmentation.canDo2: Analyze behavior changes, activity states, or regimes
timeseries-segmentation.canDo3: Build activity recognition or state-monitoring systems
timeseries-segmentation.canDo4: Train segmentation models like ClaSP or U-Net for time series
timeseries-segmentation.exampleUse: A sleep lab segments overnight EEG recordings into REM, light sleep, deep sleep, and awake stages. The model automates sleep scoring for thousands of patients.

pattern-recognition.detailAbout: Find and mark patterns that repeat across a time series — recurring shapes, motifs, or cycles. Your annotations help the model learn what these repeating structures look like so it can spot them automatically.
pattern-recognition.canDo1: Identify recurring motifs or cycles in signals
pattern-recognition.canDo2: Discover periodic patterns in financial or biomedical data
pattern-recognition.canDo3: Build pattern-matching tools for quality control
pattern-recognition.canDo4: Train models using Matrix Profile, STOMP, or SAX
pattern-recognition.exampleUse: A cardiologist marks recurring heartbeat patterns in ECG strips. The trained model flags abnormal rhythm variations across 24-hour Holter monitor recordings.

event-detection.detailAbout: Place markers at the exact moments when something important happens in a time series. Unlike segmentation (which labels ranges), event detection pinpoints discrete moments — a spike, a transition, an alert trigger.
event-detection.canDo1: Mark precise timestamps of events in sensor data
event-detection.canDo2: Detect state transitions, alerts, or change points
event-detection.canDo3: Build real-time monitoring and notification systems
event-detection.exampleUse: A seismology lab marks the exact onset of earthquake P-waves in seismograph data. The model detects earthquakes within seconds, feeding early-warning systems.

timeseries-regression.detailAbout: Attach a numeric value to specific points in a time series. Instead of categories, you're predicting numbers — a price, a temperature, a score — based on the patterns around each point.
timeseries-regression.canDo1: Label data points with continuous numeric targets
timeseries-regression.canDo2: Build pricing, valuation, or estimation models
timeseries-regression.canDo3: Predict measurements from surrounding time context
timeseries-regression.canDo4: Train regression models like XGBoost or neural networks
timeseries-regression.exampleUse: A real estate platform labels weekly housing price indices along with market indicator time series. The model predicts next-month prices for different neighborhoods.

clustering.detailAbout: Group similar time series together without predefined labels. Each series gets assigned to a cluster based on how similar its shape is to others — a great way to discover hidden groupings in your data.
clustering.canDo1: Discover natural groups in large collections of time series
clustering.canDo2: Segment customers, sensors, or signals by behavior
clustering.canDo3: Explore data before deciding on classification labels
clustering.canDo4: Train clustering models like K-Shape or DTW-KMeans
clustering.exampleUse: An IoT company groups thousands of sensor temperature curves by shape. Three natural clusters emerge: 'stable', 'daily cycle', and 'erratic' — guiding targeted maintenance strategies.

imputation.detailAbout: Mark the gaps in your time series where data is missing or unreliable. The model learns to fill those holes with plausible values, producing clean, continuous datasets ready for analysis.
imputation.canDo1: Highlight missing or corrupted data sections
imputation.canDo2: Build models that fill gaps with realistic values
imputation.canDo3: Clean sensor data for downstream analysis or training
imputation.canDo4: Train imputation models like BRITS, MICE, or KNN-based methods
imputation.exampleUse: A weather station has intermittent sensor outages. By marking the missing intervals, the imputation model fills in temperature and humidity readings, keeping the historical archive complete.

tabular.detailAbout: Work with structured data in rows and columns — CSV files, spreadsheets, or database exports. Label rows, define target columns, and prepare datasets for classical machine learning models.
tabular.canDo1: Import and label CSV or tabular datasets
tabular.canDo2: Define target variables for classification or regression
tabular.canDo3: Prepare data for models like XGBoost, Random Forest, or LightGBM
tabular.canDo4: Handle mixed data types: numbers, categories, dates
tabular.exampleUse: A bank imports a CSV of loan applications with income, credit score, and employment data. After labeling approved/denied outcomes, they train an XGBoost model that pre-screens new applications.
